concepts.title: Halogens
concepts.conceptBody:
Group 17 (Halogens)

Halogens $\left( {{\mathbf{X}}_{2}} \right)$

  • ${{\text{F}}_{2}}$ is extremely reactive, and this causes great difficulties in the preparation and handling of the element.
  • Moissan (1886) prepared it from $\text{Ca}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$:

$\text{Ca}{{\text{F}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2\text{HF}$

$\text{KF}+\text{HF}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{KH}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$

$\text{KH}{{\text{F}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{electrolysis}\underset{\text{ atcathode }}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}\,+\underset{\text{ at anode }}{\mathop{{{\text{F}}_{2}}}}\,$

  • Modern method uses graphite anode and steel cathode in a steam heated steel tank.
  • If ${{\text{F}}_{2}}$ were formed by the electrolysis of aqueous fluoride solution, it would immediately oxidise water to ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$

${{\text{F}}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}4\text{HF}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

  • The commercial production of$\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ is by electrolysis, either of concentrated aqueous $\text{NaCl}$ or of molten salt such as $\text{NaCl}(\text{l})$ or $\text{MgC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}(\text{l})$:

$2\text{NaCl}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{electrolyisis}\underbrace{2\text{NaOH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}}_{\text{at cathode }}+\underbrace{\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}}_{\text{at anode }}$

  • $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ is obtained from sea water and brine lakes. Sea water contains about $65\text{ppmB}{{\text{r}}^{\text{Br}}}\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ is isolated in following which oxidises $\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}$to $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$.

$\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+2\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}+2\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$$-$

  • Br 2 is quite volatile hence is removed by a stream of air.
  • $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ is absorbed into aq $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ solution when $\text{NaBr}$ and $\text{NaB}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ are formed:

$3\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}5\text{NaBr}+\text{NaBr}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$\text{or }\quad 3\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}+6\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}5\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}+\text{BrO}_{3}^{-}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

  • ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$ is obtainable in small quantities from dried seaweed, since certain marine plants absorb and concentrate ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$selectively in presence of $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$and $\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}$. Low concentration of ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$are also found in some natural brines (salt solutions) associated with oil fields.

Chile saltpetre is mainly $\text{NaN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ but it contains traces of sodium iodate $\text{NaI}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ and sodium periodate $\text{NaI}{{\text{O}}_{4}}.{{\text{I}}_{2}}$ is recovered by reducing $\text{IO}_{3}^{-}$to ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$by $\text{NaHS}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ and then oxidation of ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$to ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$ by $\text{IO}_{3}^{-}$itself

$\text{IO}_{3}^{-}+3\text{HSO}_{3}^{-}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{I}}^{-}}+3\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}+3{{\text{H}}^{+}}$

$5{{\text{I}}^{-}}+\text{IO}_{3}^{-}+6{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}3{{\text{I}}_{2}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Natural brine is treated with $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ water to oxidise ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$to ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$ and the solution is passed through an ion-exchange resin which absorbs ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$ as $\text{I}_{3}^{-}\cdot \text{I}_{3}^{-}$is removed from the resin by treatment with alkali.

  • Reactivity of halogens is based on bond-energy $(\text{BE})$ value.

${{\text{F}}_{2}}>\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}>\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}>{{\text{I}}_{2}}$

Greater the BE, smaller the reactivity

  • Oxidising power is in order:

${{\text{F}}_{2}}>\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}>\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}>{{\text{I}}_{2}}.$

$\frac{1}{2}{{\text{X}}_{2}}(\text{ solid, liquid, gas })\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{X}}^{-}}\text{(hydrated) }$

${{\text{F}}_{2}}$ has the most negative $\Delta \text{G}$ value, hence is strongest oxidising agent and can oxidise $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}},\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}$ and ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$to respectively $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{I}}_{2}}.{{\text{F}}_{2}}$ can also oxidise ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ to ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$.

${{\text{F}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{HF}+\frac{1}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Similarly $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ will oxidise $\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}$and ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$, and $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ will oxidise ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$. In general any halogen $\left( {{\text{X}}_{2}} \right)$ of lower atomic number will oxidise halide ions $\left( {{\text{X}}^{-}} \right)$of higher atomic number.

The ionic character of the $\text{M}-\text{X}$ bond decreases in the order $\text{M}-\text{F}>\text{M}-\text{Cl}>\text{M}-\text{Br}>\text{M}-\text{I}$

concepts.title: Reactions of Fluorine and chlorine
concepts.conceptBody:
$\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ is used as a bleach or as a disinfectant (as in public water supplies). It reacts slowly with ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ to form $\text{HCl}$ and $\text{HOCl}$. The hypochlorous acid then decomposes into $\text{HCl}$ and $\text{O}$ radicals, which kill bacteria.
$\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{HCl}+\text{HOCl }$
$\text{HOCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{HCl}+\text{O}$
These oxygen radicals are very strong oxidising as well as effective bleaching and disinfecting agent in aqueous solution of $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ or hypochlorite salts.

  • The halogens react with each other to form interhalogen compounds of the type $\text{AX},\text{A}{{\text{X}}_{3}},\text{A}{{\text{X}}_{5}}$ and $\text{A}{{\text{X}}_{7}}$ of which $\text{A}$ is of higher atomic number as shown:
  • There are never more than two different halogens in a molecule. The bonds are covalent because of the small electronegativity difference. Ternary combinations occur in polyhalide ions (e.g., $\text{IBrC}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$)
  • The electrical conductance of molten ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$ is ascribed to self-ionisation

$3{{\text{I}}_{2}}\rightleftharpoons \text{I}_{3}^{+}+\text{I}_{3}^{-}$

  • The interhalogens are generally more reactive than the halogens (except${{\text{F}}_{2}}$). This is because the$\text{A}-\text{X}$ bond in interhalogens is weaker than the $\text{X}-\text{X}$ bond in the halogens.
  • Hydrolysis of interhalogens gives halide and oxohalide. Oxohalide is always formed from larger halogens present.

$\text{Br}{{\text{F}}_{5}}\xrightarrow{{{H}_{2}}O}5~{{\text{F}}^{-}}+\underset{\text{ bromate ion }}{\mathop{\text{BrO}_{3}^{-}}}\,$
$\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{{H}_{2}}O}\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}+\underset{\text{ iodate ion }}{\mathop{\text{IO}_{3}^{-}}}\,$

concepts.title: Anomalous Behaviour of fluorine
concepts.conceptBody:
Anomalous Behaviour of Fluorine

Fluorine differs from other elements of the group because of its exceptionally small atomic and ionic size and low fluorine-fluorine bond dissociation energy. The result of these differences is that fluorine is the most electronegative element in the periodic table and is a powerful oxidant. Some differences between flourine and other halogens are:

  • It is more reactive than other halogens because of low F-F bond energy, also due to its high electronegativity the bond between fluorine and other elements is very strong so its compounds are more stable. Some of them are inert. e.g. $\text{S}{{\text{F}}_{6}}$.
  • Fluorine is almost invariably monocoordinate (coordination number $=1$ ) and is never more than mono covalent.
  • Fluorides are more ionic.
  • Fluorine forms strong hydrogen bonds resulting in the properties of hydrogen fluoride being anomalous.
  • Fluorine is the strongest oxidising agent and oxidises the elements to their highest oxidation state, for example in $\text{I}{{\text{F}}_{7}}$, iodine has oxidation number seven.
  • The reactions of fluorine are also different from other halogens. Fluorine fumes in moist air and decomposes water to give oxygen, whereas other halogens are sparingly soluble in water and react partly to give hydrohalic and hypohalous acids.

$2~{{\text{F}}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}4\text{HF}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

${{\text{X}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{HOX}+\text{HX}$

Fluorine reacts with alkalies to yield the oxide, ${{\text{F}}_{2}}\text{O}$:

$2~{{\text{F}}_{2}}+2\text{NaOH}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{O}{{\text{F}}_{2}}+2\text{NaF}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

The other halogens react with alkalies to yield a solution of hypohalite ions, which may disproportionate. The oxoacid of fluorine, $\text{HOF}$, prepared recently is very unstable. The oxides of fluorine are not acidic.

$\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+2\text{NaOH}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{NaClO}+\text{NaCl}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

concepts.title: Halogen acids
concepts.conceptBody:
Halogen Acids

  • All the halogens acids (HF,$\text{HCl},\text{HBr},\text{HI})$ are known. In aqueous solution, they are typical mineral acids whose acid strengths decrease in the direction of increasing bond energy, that is

$\underbrace{\text{HI}>\text{HBr}>\text{HCl}}_{\text{strong acids }}>\underbrace{\text{HF}}_{\text{weak acid }}$

  • $\text{HX}$ can be prepared by direct combination of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{X}}_{2}}$

${{\text{H}}_{2}}(~\text{g})+{{\text{X}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{HX}(\text{g})$

  • reaction between${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{F}}_{2}}$ is very fast,
  • reaction between ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ and $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ is also rapid,
  • but reaction between ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ and $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ or ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$ is very slow.

$\Delta \text{G}_{\text{f}}^{{}^\circ }$ of $\text{HF},\text{HCl}$ and $\text{HBr}$ are large and negative suggesting that for them reaction

${{\text{H}}_{2}}+{{\text{X}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{HX}$

goes to completion. For $\text{HI}(\text{g}),\Delta \text{G}_{\text{f}}^{{}^\circ }$ is small and positive suggesting that $\text{HI}$ should dissociate

$2\text{HI}(\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}(~\text{g})+{{\text{I}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$

to some extent. However, because of the high activation energy the dissociation occurs only very slowly in the absence of a catalyst. As a result, $\text{HI}(\text{g})$ is quite stable at room temperature. Thus, at room temperature decomposition of $\text{HI}(\text{g})$ is kinetically controlled (rather than thermodynamically controlled).

  • $\text{Ca}{{\text{F}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{CaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2\text{HF}$
  • $\text{Ca}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$ has also $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ as impurity otherwise. $\text{HF}$ formed would react with $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ forming $\text{Si}{{\text{F}}_{4}}$ and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Si}{{\text{F}}_{6}}$.

$\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+4\text{HF}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Si}{{\text{F}}_{4}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{Si}{{\text{F}}_{4}}+2\text{HF}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\left[ \text{Si}{{\text{F}}_{6}} \right]$

  • $\text{NaCl}+\underset{\text{conc}\text{.}}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}}\,\xrightarrow{150{}^\circ C}\text{HCl}+\underset{\text{salt cake}}{\mathop{\text{NaHS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}}\,$

$\text{NaHS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+\text{NaCl}\xrightarrow{550{}^\circ C}\text{HCl}+\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

This method is called 'salt-cake' method involves formation of$\text{NaHS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$(salt cake)

  • $\text{HF}$ is only just liquid at room temperature and $\text{HCl},\text{HBr}$ and $\text{HI}$ are gases. $\text{HF}$ has abnormally high boiling point due to intermolecular H-bonding which is not possible in other halogen acids due to their smaller electronegativity values.

$\text{HCl}<\text{HBr}<\text{HI}<\text{HF}$

Some H-bonding also occurs in the gas, which consists of a mixture of cyclic${{(\text{HF})}_{6}}$ polymers, dimeric ${{(\text{HF})}_{2}}$ and the monomeric $\text{HF}$.

Because of the tendency to form hydrogen bond, metal fluorides are solvated by HF giving species of the type $\text{HF}_{2}^{-}$etc. $\text{KH}{{\text{F}}_{2}}(\text{KF}.\text{HF}),\text{K}{{\text{H}}_{2}}~{{\text{F}}_{3}}(\text{KF}.2\text{HF})$ and $\text{K}{{\text{H}}_{3}}~{{\text{F}}_{4}}(\text{KF}.3\text{HF})$ are known examples.

  • HF attacks glass, other halogen acids do not. Glass is silicates of sodium and calcium and are converted into complex fluorides:

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+6\text{HF}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Si}{{\text{F}}_{6}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{CaSi}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+6\text{HF}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaSi}{{\text{F}}_{6}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Hence HF can't be stored in a glass vessel. Instead, it is stored in a wax-vessel which does not react with HF. This unique property of$\text{HF}$of attacking glass is used for making permanent marking eg. glass-wares.

  • In the gaseous state, HX are essentially covalent. In aqueous solution, they ionise to form${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$and ${{\text{X}}^{-}}$but not ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$and ${{\text{X}}^{-}}$.

$\underset{\text{ base }}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}}}\,+\underset{\text{ acid }}{\mathop{\text{HX}}}\,\rightleftharpoons \underset{\text{ acid }}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}}}\,+\underset{\text{ base }}{\mathop{{{\text{X}}^{-}}}}\,$

HF has more ionic character (due to greater electronegativity difference) than HCl, HBr and HI but acid strength varies as

$\underset{\text{ weak }}{\mathop{\text{HF}}}\,<\underbrace{\text{HCl}<\text{HBr}<\text{HI}}_{\text{strong }}$

Acid strength is dependent not on the electronegativity difference of $\text{H}$ and $\text{X}$ but on the tendency of the hydrated molecule to form hydrogen ions:

$\text{HX (hydrated) }\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}^{+}}\text{(hydrated) }+{{\text{X}}^{-}}\text{(hydrated) }$

  • Dipole moment of HF is maximum and is in order

$\underset{1.19\text{D}}{\mathop{\text{HF}}}\,>\underset{1.05\text{D}}{\mathop{\text{HCl}}}\,>\underset{0.80\text{D}}{\mathop{\text{HBr}}}\,>\underset{0.420\text{D}}{\mathop{\text{HI}}}\,$

concepts.title: Oxoacids of Halogens
concepts.conceptBody:
Preparations of oxo acids

Most of the oxo acids and acid anions of halogens are known only in solution. They can be obtained by reaction of the free halogens with (i) water or (ii) aq. bases:

${{\text{X}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}^{+}}+{{\text{X}}^{-}}+\text{HOX}$

${{\text{X}}_{2}}+\underset{\text{ (cold dil}\text{. })}{\mathop{2\text{NaOH}}}\,\xrightarrow{{}}\text{NaX}+\text{NaOX}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$3{{\text{X}}_{2}}+\underset{(\text{ conc}\text{. hot })}{\mathop{6\text{NaOH}}}\,\xrightarrow{{}}5\text{NaX}+\text{NaOX}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$(\text{X}=\text{Cl},\text{Br},\text{I})$

All the above reactions are rapid disproportionation of halogens.

Note: 

(i) Reaction of ${{\text{X}}_{2}}$ and water can produce HOX only.

(ii) There is appreciable concentration of HOCl.

(iii) The HOI is negligible in a saturated solution of iodine.

Halic acids $\mathbf{HOX}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{(X=Cl,Br,I)}$

All the three halic acids are known. The acids $\text{HOCl}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ and $\text{HOBr}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ are known only in solution and as salts. Only iodic acid $\left( \text{HOI}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right)$ is stable out of aq. solution. It is a white solid. It can be prepared by oxidizing ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$ with conc. $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ or ${{\text{O}}_{3}}$.

${{\text{I}}_{2}}+10\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{HI}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+10\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\mathbf{HOCl}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$ and $\mathbf{HOBr}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$

They can be made by treating barium halates with ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

$\text{Ba}{{\left( \text{Cl}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{HCl}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{BaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\downarrow $

$\text{Ba}{{\left( \text{Cl}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}$ can be prepared by passing $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ in $\text{Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}$ solution

$6\text{Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+6\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Ba}{{\left( \text{Cl}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+5\text{BaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

They can also be produced by reactions of ${{\text{X}}_{2}}$ with hot aq. base $(\text{NaOH})$

$3{{\text{X}}_{2}}+6\text{H}{{\text{O}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{XO}_{3}^{-}+5{{\text{X}}^{-}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{KCl}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\text{ decomposes on heating, the product depends on temperature }$

(i)

(ii) $4\text{KCl}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow[Temp.]{Lower}3\text{KCl}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+\text{KCl}$

$\text{Zn}{{\left( \text{Cl}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }2\text{ZnO}+2\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+5{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Chlorates are used in matches and fireworks. Sodium chlorate is used as a powerful weed killer.

Perbromic acid $\mathbf{HOBr}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$

It is best prepared by oxidation of $\text{BrO}_{3}^{-}$by ${{\text{F}}_{2}}$ in $5\text{MNaOH}$ solution.

$\text{BrO}_{3}^{-}+{{\text{F}}_{2}}+2\text{H}{{\text{O}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{BrO}_{4}^{-}+2~{{\text{F}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Solution of $\text{HBr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ can be concentrated upto $55%(6\text{M})$, without decomposition. The hydrate $\text{HBr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot 2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ can be crystallized.

Periodic acid $\mathbf{HOI}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$

The common form in aq. solution of this acid is $\text{HI}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ and also ${{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{I}{{\text{O}}_{6}}\cdot {{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{I}{{\text{O}}_{6}}$ is called paraperiodic acid (or orthoperiodic acid). It is a white crystal. It is decomposes on heating as

$2{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{I}{{\text{O}}_{6}}\frac{{{100}^{{}^\circ }}\text{C}}{\left( -4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}2\text{HI}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{\sim 200{}^\circ C}{{\text{I}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{5}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

The $\text{IO}_{4}^{-}$ion is tetrahedral while ${{(\text{OH})}_{5}}\text{IO}$ is octahedral. Periodates can be made by oxiding iodate with $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ in alkaline solution.

$\text{IO}_{3}^{-}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+6\text{H}{{\text{O}}^{-}}\to \text{IO}_{6}^{5-}+2\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Structure of oxo anions

Structure and Shape

concepts.title: Pseudohalogens
concepts.conceptBody:
Pseudohalogens and Pseudohalides

Many substances have properties either like ${{\text{X}}_{2}}$ or ${{\text{X}}^{-}}(\text{X}=$ halogen). They are called pseudohalogens and pseudohalides. They contain two or more atoms in which one at least is $\text{N}-$ atom, such as ${{(\text{CN})}_{2}},\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}},\text{SC}{{\text{N}}^{-}}$etc.

Some pseudohalogens and pseudohalides 

The cyanogen gas and $\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}}$ion are best-known pseudohalogen and pseudohalide. They behave like ${{\text{X}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{X}}^{-}}$, e.g.,

(a) ${{(\text{CN})}_{2}}$ disproportionate in water like ${{\text{X}}_{2}}$

${{(\text{CN})}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\to \text{HCN}+\text{HOCN}$

$\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\to \text{HCl}+\text{HOCl}$

(b) $\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}}$behaves similar to ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$

$2\text{Cu}_{(\text{aq})}^{2+}+4\text{CN}_{(\text{aq})}^{-}\to 2\text{CuCN}+{{(\text{CN})}_{2}}$

$2\text{Cu}_{(\text{aq})}^{2+}+4{{\text{I}}^{-}}\to 2\text{CuI}+{{\text{I}}_{2}}$

Both the above reactions are reduction of $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}$ to $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{+}}$by $\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}}$and ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$ions.

(c) The solubility of metal halides and metal cyanides are very similar,

$\text{NaX}$ and $\text{NaCN}\to $ Water-soluble

$\text{AgX}$ and $\text{AgCN}\to $ Water-insoluble

$(\text{X}=\text{Cl},\text{Br},\text{I})$

$\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ and $\text{Pb}{{(\text{CN})}_{2}}\to $ Water insoluble

(d) Like halides pseudohalides $\left( \text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}},\text{SC}{{\text{N}}^{-}} \right.$etc. )are good ligands $\text{CuCl}_{4}^{2-},\text{ZnCl}_{4}^{2-},\text{Cu}(\text{CN})_{4}^{2-}$. \{Please note, the word ligands will be dealt in chapter co-ordination complexes\}

(i) $\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}}$complexes with transition metals and Zn, Cd and Hg.

(ii) Most of the$\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}}$complexes are ionic ${{\left[ \text{Fe}{{(\text{CN})}_{6}} \right]}^{4-}},{{\left[ \text{Ni}{{(\text{CN})}_{4}} \right]}^{2-}},{{\left[ \text{Mn}{{(\text{CN})}_{6}} \right]}^{4-}}$ etc.

(iii) $\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}}$can stabilize low oxidation states also, e.g ${{\left[ \text{Ni}{{(\text{CN})}_{4}} \right]}^{4-}}$.

concepts.title: Bleaching Powder
concepts.conceptBody:
Bleaching Powder

The exact chemical composition of bleaching powder is not yet known but it behaves as if it contains calcium hypochlorite $\text{Ca}{{(\text{OCl})}_{2}}$ and basic calcium chloride, $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\cdot \text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}\cdot {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Preparation 

It is prepared by passing chlorine over slaked lime

$3\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+2\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underbrace{\text{Ca}{{(\text{OCl})}_{2}}+\text{CaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\cdot \text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}\cdot {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}}_{\text{Bleaching Powder }}$

Properties: 

1. Reaction with dilute acids: With dilute acids, it gives chlorine which is known as available chlorine.

$\text{CaOC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+2\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\uparrow $

$\text{CaOC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\uparrow $

2. When treated with water it decomposes into calcium chloride and calcium hypochlorite.

$2\text{CaOC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\text{Ca}{{(\text{OCl})}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

3. Bleaching powder reacts with $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ (atmospheric) and gives chlorine which accounts for its oxidising and bleaching actions.

$\text{CaOC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\uparrow $

4. Action of heat: On heating bleaching powder gives a mixture of chlorate and chloride.

$6\text{CaOC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{Ca}{{\left( \text{Cl}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+5\text{CaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$
